FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, Baseline N = 11,878, 4 waves of longitudinal data (Years 1-4)

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership measured as yes/no from parent report; age of smartphone acquisition tracked across waves

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel mixed-effects models testing smartphone ownership predicting parent-child informant discrepancy in internalizing problems; Time × Informant interactions; inferential statistical tests reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple coefficients reported: b = -0.21, SE = .042, p < .001, 95% CI [-.29, -.23] for parent-child discrepancy; Table 3 reports extensive parameter estimates with SE and p-values across smartphone age groups

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis from ABCD Study with 4 waves; mixed-effects and multilevel modeling

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Development and Psychopathology (2025), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership is the independent variable predicting parent-child discrepancy (non-SM outcome); does not meet FT-EC7 criteria which require SM as dependent variable with only demographic predictors in longitudinal analyses
notes: Clear longitudinal study with smartphone as IV and parental perception of child mental health as outcome; meets all inclusion criteria

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study, T1: N = 11,787; longitudinal design across 3 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures electronic media use (EMU) with specific types: social electronic media, video games, video viewing; also reports overall EMU composite via Screen Time Questionnaire

FT_IC3.evidence: Bidirectional longitudinal associations tested via random-intercept crosslagged panel models with reported coefficients and significance tests; separate models for total EMU and specific media types

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple coefficients reported: total EMU→family conflict β = 0.10, p = .023; social EMU (girls) T1→T2 β = 0.13, p = .008; family conflict→social EMU T2→T3 β = -0.59, p < .001; Table 1 displays correlations and statistics

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis with random-intercept crosslagged panel models across 3 years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Family Psychology (2025), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Reports bidirectional associations (EMU→family conflict AND family conflict→EMU); includes cross-sectional and longitudinal components; family conflict is not solely a demographic predictor; does not meet FT-EC7 criteria
notes: Electronic media includes social media component which qualifies under FT-IC2; bidirectional design with specific media types separately analyzed

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study, Baseline N ~12,000 (M_age = 9.9); 4-year longitudinal follow-up with 3-4 assessment waves

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via self-reported daily hours on weekday and weekend; specific platforms mentioned (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, etc.); log-transformed to approximate normal distribution

FT_IC3.evidence: Parallel process latent growth curve models testing associations between social media use trajectories and cognitive performance trajectories; reported intercept-slope associations and slope-slope associations with test statistics

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple coefficients with p-values reported: icryst ↔ ism = -0.09, p < .001; ipattern ↔ ism; Table 3 reports extensive parameter estimates: icryst ↔ ism β = -0.36, p < .001; various slope associations with p-values; Table 2 reports univariate growth model estimates with SE and 95% CI

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis using parallel process latent growth curve models across 4 years with multiple cognitive domains

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Research on Adolescence (2025), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Social media use is the independent variable with cognitive performance trajectories (crystallized abilities, inhibitory control/attention, processing speed) as outcomes (non-SM outcomes); does not meet FT-EC7 criteria
notes: Latent growth curve analysis with social media-specific pathway extractable from models; uses domain-specific cognitive outcomes demonstrating clear SM→non-SM associations
